description: Apricots are the most climate-challenged stone fruit in Toronto, as their extremely early bloom period makes them vulnerable to late spring frosts that frequently destroy the crop. Despite this, apricot trees grow vigorously in Toronto's Zone 6a climate and produce abundant fruit in years with mild springs. They are most successful when planted against south-facing walls or in other sheltered microclimates that protect blossoms from late April cold snaps. Hardy cultivars developed for northern growing, such as 'Harcot' and 'Harlayne' from the Harrow research station in Ontario, offer the best chances of reliable fruiting.

identification.leaves: Apricot leaves are broadly ovate to heart-shaped, 5–9 cm long, with a pointed tip and finely serrated margins. They are glossy dark green with a smooth upper surface and turn yellow in autumn.
identification.bark: Young apricot bark is smooth and reddish-brown, becoming darker and deeply furrowed on mature trees. Older bark develops a rough, plated texture and frequently exudes translucent amber-coloured gum.
identification.flowers: Apricot flowers are among the earliest to bloom in Toronto, appearing in mid to late April before the leaves on bare branches. Flowers are solitary, 2–3 cm across, white to pale pink with a sweet almond fragrance.
identification.fruit: Apricot fruits are round to slightly oblong drupes, 4–6 cm in diameter, with velvety golden-orange skin often blushed with red. The flesh is aromatic, sweet-tart, and orange, surrounding a smooth stone that separates easily in most cultivars.

ripening: Apricots ripen in Toronto from mid-July to early August, making them one of the earliest stone fruits to mature. The fruit ripens quickly over a 1–2 week period and must be monitored closely to avoid loss to splitting or bird damage. Fruit is ready when it develops full golden colour and yields to gentle pressure.

foraging.bestTime: Harvest apricots in mid to late July when they are fully coloured golden-orange and separate easily from the branch. Check trees daily during ripening as the window between underripe and overripe is very short, often just 2–3 days per fruit.
foraging.storage: Ripe apricots are extremely perishable, lasting only 2–3 days at room temperature or up to a week refrigerated. They dry exceptionally well (traditionally halved and sun-dried), freeze adequately, and make outstanding jam and preserves.
foraging.uses: Fresh apricots at peak ripeness are intensely flavoured and aromatic, excellent in tarts, compotes, and eaten out of hand. Dried apricots are a concentrated nutrient source, and apricot jam is considered one of the finest stone fruit preserves. The kernels can be used sparingly as a flavouring (similar to bitter almond) in baking.
foraging.safety: Apricot kernels contain significant amounts of amygdalin and should not be eaten in quantity—Health Canada recommends no more than 3 kernels per day for adults. The fruit flesh is completely safe and highly nutritious, rich in beta-carotene and vitamin A.

ecologicalValue: Apricot's very early bloom provides critical nectar for the first emerging bees of spring in Toronto, including bumblebee queens establishing new colonies. Their fruit ripens early enough to feed birds and mammals during the mid-summer period when other food sources may be scarce. As relatively small trees, they fit well in urban gardens and contribute to the diversity of the city's edible urban forest.
